design_philosophy.core_loop: 2v2 full-court basketball with emphasis on physical confrontation: score, defend, punch, steal, repeat
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward of aggressive play: punching and tackling opponents for ball control vs. maintaining positioning for scoring opportunities
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Street-level competitive basketball where physicality and brawling are legitimate tactics; 'playground ball' attitude rather than professional rule-bound play
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Explicitly branded as 'A Basket Brawl' - the game's core opinion is that basketball should reward aggression and physical play. Punching has no penalty; only shot clock violations are called. Chaos on court (soda cans, candy wrappers, referee obstacles) is celebrated as core mechanic rather than avoided. Forerunner to NBA Jam's arcade sensibility.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Game structure is match-based (best-of-4 quarters, sudden death overtime). Difficulty emerges from opponent AI response to aggressive play and court hazard navigation rather than explicit difficulty settings.

progression.unlock_mechanism: 8 playable characters with unique characteristics can be selected; teams randomized per match
replayability_hooks: Character selection affects playstyle (each character has unique trait)
Randomized team matchups change opponent behavior
Arcade version allows 'Hometown Heroes' operator customization

world_building_techniques: Fictional team names with regional identity (Chicago, Los Angeles, New York, Denver, Natural High, Brawl State)

faction_indicators: Team selection creates implicit home/away dynamic (Player 1 = home team, Player 2 = away team)
Regional team names (Chicago, LA, NY, Denver) suggest territorial affiliation
danger_signals: Punching mechanic with no penalty creates permission structure for physical aggression
Court hazards (soda cans, candy wrappers) represent environmental chaos/disrespect
Short pants-pulling animation in arcade version escalates mockery mechanics
environmental_storytelling: Litter on court (soda cans, candy wrappers) signals street-level/informal setting vs professional arena
Referee present but powerless except for shot clock - suggests informal rules enforcement
Court layout and two-player-per-team structure evokes playground basketball aesthetic
